item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
The first image is owned by Oren Jeffries, which is not under an explicit Creative Commons license.

Last two images could not be sourced reliably and are assumed not compliant with CC 3.0.

All images have since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love how it doesn't just have some anomalous mushroom, it also shows the effect of the scip on the town before the foundation found it, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: DisquietRiot is right, but I still consider this scip is good enough to warrant an upvote (Congrats on the +100, by the way!). Maybe it's not a realistic depiction of a town beset by the supernatural, but it sure is an interesting one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
See… I disagree. I feel like the human reactions were not reasonable, and this is what kept me from an upvote (currently no/neutral vote). The "Daylight Hunters" were quasi-believable, especially the paranoid and insular nature of the group.

Howevet… who in their right mind would work with genital-consuming shadow blobs that turn people into corpse-puppets? These things are cartoonishly over the top evil, and I don't understand why a cult popped up to aid them.

The Enders also seemed shallow to me. Their philosophy seemed pretty flimsy, and the concept of "neutrality", as defined in Dungeons and Dragons terms, always seemed unrealistic to me. Preserving the "balance of good and evil" really doesn't make a lot of sense. People actively promoting evil or neutrality (neutrality in the cosmic sense) always seemed kind of stupid and unrealistic to me. It's like the orcs of D&D saying, "we're going to summon our god/burn the castle/sacrifice the virgin so evil can triumph!" I mean… why? What's their motivation for actively promoting evil as a concept (or, in the case on "neutrality", arguing that "evil" has a right to exist).

No, that's not how people think, and not how religion works. People instead contextualize their actions as "good" or at least "necessary". Nazis are scary not because they do terrible things, but because they do terrible things that, in their warped worldview, are necessary and right. That's how evil in human nature actually functions.

TL;DR- I strongly disagree that this is a realistic depiction of regular people reacting to anomalies, and may downvote for this reason.

EDIT: After sitting on this for a few days, I've decided my reasons for disliking this are strong enough to constitute a downvote, even if I'm downvoting for largely personal reasons. Sorry, man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Intriguing anomaly and a nice projection of how regular people would react to anomalous activity. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As part of a larger initiative, these members also actively destroyed all methods of communication, including phones and internet, as well as ensuring no citizens left the city after the initial exposure to the anomaly in order to halt the spread of SCP-1627-B outside of the town.

Wouldn't people besides the Foundation (like family members and friends) have noticed the whole town suddenly going incommunicado?

item.title: Re: Hmmm
item.content:encoded: The fungus itself is fairly easy to contain, and the ritualistic behavior it requires to activate its effects isn't likely to be accidentally discovered very often. Besides that, this particular species hasn't been found anywhere other than this area yet. As for the -A's and -B's, they can be handled by Foundation employees effectively and quickly because they know what they're dealing with and how to contain them.

item.title: Hmmm
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't the object class be keter? I mean, they need a complicated and large array of protocalls to contain the fungus, and its pretty dangerous.

item.title: Re: consistency question
item.content:encoded:
They do not need food in the traditional sense of the word. However,

When a human being comes into direct skin contact with an instance of SCP-1627-A, all damage and infections present within them will be healed. Over the course of the following two (2) weeks, that subject will experience rapid senescence until death, often by natural causes associated with old age. This appears to give nourishment to the SCP-1627-A instance.

So, they don't need food and water ever, but if they go three or more weeks without touching someone, they die due to lack of nourishment. Comprendez-vous?

item.title: consistency question
item.content:encoded: In the explanation of -A it says the instance does not need food for substance. Later the article then says that -A and -B instances can be starved after 3 weeks. Is this a mistake, or are they referring to other types of sustenance?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fly agaric is commonly used for it's psychotropic properites.
I could imagine someone stumbling upon this little colony of what they think are "magic mushrooms" and harvesting them for fun and/or profit. It's pretty common for halluciongens to be consumed somewhere semi-private or at least away from the general public, to reduce risk of getting caught; there isn't a lot of direct sunlight in your average stoner basement/attic/living room/rave. The only unusual aspect for the first activation is the blood, and people "under the influence" are more prone to accidentally injuring themselves. Since the article isn't specific, it might only take a nasty papercut.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Glad to see I'm not the only one. I was totally expecting him to have wrote it.

Well, it's part of the mythos in my heart.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You did a really fantastic job at realizing this, and I'm impressed. You had a difficult hand dealt to you and you pulled it off without missing a step.

I do have to agree that the ritual procedures seem kind of like a stretch in regards to discovery, but I guess with the killing off of the first cult you can't really elaborate on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that worked well. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As interesting as this is, the way it is activated kind of bugs me regarding who would do that. As I have said, though, this is a good SCP. I think those groups are humorous in a dark way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The GoIs exist specifically for this skip and this skip only. The point of it was to show human reaction to something anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought of that too, but the piece started to feel less like something of his afterwards.

(It would also be super cool if other people started using that stuff because I love it)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This feels kinda like part of sandrewswann's He Who Made Dark/He Who Made Light mythos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think the GOIs are meant to be expanded upon beyond the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is just kind of weird. Mushrooms whose effects are normal unless you do something in particular is a neat idea, I just can't say I'm terribly impressed by the results. You either get immortals who suck the squishy stuff out of people, or immortals who are made of squishy stuff. The rest seems like an attempt to shoehorn three new GoIs into an article, which doesn't seem like a great idea, really.

In all, I'm not sure what's really intended by this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was hoping that second picture would remain unused.

Oh well.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read this in chat and I liked it then. Now, well, I love it. Great job, Azzle

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is flipping fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Having just reviewed it just over an hour ago, I'm quite happy to upvote it as it is. Again, very creative and everything was tied together quite well.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Done in response to Wilt's challenge, given the tags #fungus, #ritual, and #medical and the first and last pictures.

EDIT: PLEASE NOTE I WAS BEING A STUPIDHEAD AND FORGOT THAT WILT'S CHALLENGE THINGS WEREN'T SUPPOSED TO BE POSTED YET. This being the case, I might delete this soon and repost it when I'm supposed to but I have to go to a thing now for six hours ._. so, uh, yeah, if you're doing the challenge, don't follow my example.
